title: Growing strawberries
subtitle: A bed that fruits for three years, replanted from its own runners
excerpt: Strawberry plants (Fragaria × ananassa) crop for three productive years before the yield drops. The standard British strawberry bed is planted in autumn, fruits the next June, and propagates itself through summer runners. A walk-through of the bed cycle, with notes on container culture for balcony gardeners.
sourceNotes: RHS strawberry page (https://www.rhs.org.uk/fruit/strawberries/grow-your-own) for the planting window, runner-management cadence, and netting practice. Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), Project Gutenberg, for the three-year replanting cycle described as standard practice. William Robinson, The English Flower Garden (1883 edition), Project Gutenberg, for the original strawberry-bed culture and the runner-removal discipline.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal stem a strawberry plant sends out in summer. The runner rests on the soil and roots at each node, producing a new clone of the parent. Runners are the strawberry bed's propagation route; the gardener pins them down where a new plant is wanted and snips them off elsewhere.

A strawberry variety that produces one heavy crop in early summer (around mid-June in temperate UK). Distinct from ever-bearing or day-neutral varieties, which crop in lighter waves through the whole growing season.

content.text: Strawberries are the fruit a UK garden can rely on. A bed of twelve plants set in October will give a family three kilos of fruit the following June, six kilos by year two, and four kilos by year three before the yield drops and the bed needs replanting. The plant is hardy, self-fertile, and propagates itself through the summer — the bed renews itself from its own runners if you let three or four root each year.

This guide covers the full bed cycle: choosing a position, planting in autumn or early spring, the year-one fruiting season, runner management, and the three-year replant. Container culture for balcony and patio gardeners follows the same rhythm at smaller scale.
Choosing a position

Six hours of direct sun a day is the threshold for a worthwhile crop. South or west-facing beds carry the strongest yield; east-facing beds crop on the lighter side; north-facing beds and deeply-shaded spots are not worth the work. Strawberries grow on most soils but won't tolerate wet feet — a heavy clay site needs a raised bed or generous grit added at planting.

Avoid following potatoes, tomatoes, or peppers in the same bed — they share verticillium wilt, and strawberries planted into a wilt-infected bed crop weakly and short-lived. A four-year gap is the standard rotation guidance.
Planting out

Two planting windows. Autumn (September and October) produces the heaviest first-year crop because the plants establish over winter and arrive at June with strong roots. Early spring (March and early April) works for plants bought in growing condition, but the first-year crop is lighter. Bare-root runners are the cheapest option; pot-grown plants are the simplest.

Set the plants 45 cm apart in rows 75 cm apart, or 45 cm apart in a square grid if the bed is small. The crown (the central point where the leaves emerge) sits at soil level — buried, the crown rots; perched too high, the new roots dry out. Firm the soil around the roots and water in.

Container culture: 25 cm pots, one plant per pot, in a loam-based compost (John Innes No 2 is the classic UK choice). Stacking strawberry planters with side pockets work but the lower plants run dry quickly — water every day in summer. Hanging baskets work for one plant per basket and look pretty; the yield is modest.
Through the season

Late April through May: the plants come out of winter dormancy and put up the first leaves and flower trusses. Watering is moderate — the soil should be evenly moist but not waterlogged. A light feed of liquid seaweed or tomato food once flowers form helps fruit set.

Mid-May: as the first fruits start to set, tuck a layer of clean straw under each plant. Straw keeps the developing fruit off the soil (the original etymology of the name) and reduces slug damage and grey mould. Black plastic mulch works as a modern alternative; clean garden compost works less well because the soft surface holds moisture against the fruit.

Early June: net the bed before the first fruits start to colour. Blackbirds and pigeons strip a strawberry bed in a single morning otherwise. A simple frame of canes with horticultural netting draped over and pegged at the base is enough; check daily for trapped birds and unhook the netting after the picking is over so the bed dries through July.

After the harvest finishes in early July: cut the old leaves back to about 10 cm above the crown. The plant puts on a flush of fresh leaves through August and September that builds the crown for the following year. This is also when the plants send out
runners
— long horizontal stems with small plantlets at each node.
Runner management and the three-year cycle

Runner management is the lever between a strawberry bed that produces fruit and one that produces only foliage. Every runner the plant supports is energy not going to the next year's crop. The standard approach: let the parent plant produce one or two runners; pin the first plantlet of each runner into a pot of compost (a plastic pot sunk into the bed alongside the parent works); snip everything beyond that plantlet off. Once the plantlet has rooted into the pot (four to six weeks), sever the runner connecting it to the parent and lift the pot.

Three years is the productive life of a strawberry plant. Year one carries a moderate crop; year two is the peak; year three is solid but declining; year four onwards drops to half the year-two yield, and the disease load (mildew, viruses) climbs. The standard practice is a rolling three-year replant: plant the bed in autumn year 0, propagate runners in summer year 2, plant the runners into a fresh bed in autumn year 2, dig out the original bed at the end of year 3.
Harvest
A
June-bearing

strawberry is ready when the fruit has coloured fully (no white shoulders), yields slightly to gentle pressure, and lifts cleanly off the stem with the green cap (the calyx) still attached. Pick in the cool of the morning where possible — strawberries picked warm bruise more easily and don't keep as well. A fully-ripe strawberry doesn't store more than two or three days even in the fridge; eat fresh or convert to jam, freezer purée, or chutney within 48 hours.

Yield expectations: 250-500 g per plant per year for a mature June-bearing variety, more for ever-bearers spread over the season but less concentrated. Twelve plants gives a family-of-four a couple of weeks of fresh strawberries plus a kilo or two for the jam pot.
What can go wrong

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The common ways a UK strawberry bed under-performs, and the fix.

items.symptom: Foliage strong, fruit small and few
items.cause: Too much nitrogen (over-rich compost or lawn-fertiliser drift), or runners not removed from the parent.
items.fix: Cut runners back ruthlessly. Switch to a high-potash feed when flowers form, not a balanced one.

items.symptom: Fruit rotting on the plant before ripening
items.cause: Wet weather plus contact with damp soil; grey mould (Botrytis cinerea).
items.fix: Straw mulch under every plant. Pick ripe fruit promptly: over-ripe fruit left on the plant becomes the infection source for the rest. Ventilation matters; don't over-crowd plants.

items.cause: Net not in place before fruit starts colouring.
items.fix: Net mid-May at the latest. Once birds have learned the bed, the loss compounds. Bird-scaring tape is a second-line measure; netting is the first.

items.cause: Crowns planted too deep, or wet soil through winter.
items.fix: Replant the survivors with the crown at soil level. Raise the bed on heavy clay sites. Add grit to the planting hole on persistent waterlogged ground.

items.symptom: Whole bed declining in year four
items.cause: Normal end of productive life — disease load up, vigour down.
items.fix: Plan the three-year rolling replant. Propagate runners in year 2, plant the new bed in autumn of year 2, dig out the old bed at the end of year 3.

content.text: Variety pointers

For a UK first bed, start with Cambridge Favourite (the established mid-season June-bearer, reliable everywhere) or Honeoye (the early-season standard). Add a late-season variety for a longer fresh-fruit window — Florence is a UK classic. Ever-bearing options like Mara des Bois extend the picking from June to October but yield in lighter waves. The variety-selection guide for strawberries covers the named-variety set in depth.
